title: Polymer Knots in Thin Films: Thickness Dependence, Local Effects, and Stiffness
authors: Maurice P. Schmitt, Hendrik Meyer, Peter Virnau
abstract: We study how confinement affects topology and conformations in polymer films of varying thickness $h$. The knotting probability exhibits a maximum at intermediate thicknesses near the bulk radius of gyration $h \approx R_\mathrm{g,bulk}$, vanishes at small $h$ and approaches bulk values for large $h$. Close to walls, the entanglement length increases monotonically and conformations become flatter. A layer-resolved analysis of structural and topological properties allows us to reconstruct the explicit thickness dependencies by integrating layer-resolved properties of a thick film.
summary: This work quantitatively examines how confining a polymer film between two walls affects its internal structure and topology. Key findings show that knotting probability peaks at an intermediate thickness related to the chain's bulk size, and that wall proximity causes the polymer conformation to flatten. The study uses advanced simulation techniques and layer-by-layer analysis to build a comprehensive picture linking confinement, chain flexibility, and topological complexity.
main_problem: The study investigates how physical confinement, modeled by varying film thickness, alters the topological properties (knotting) and conformational statistics (entanglements) of polymer chains.
main_result: Knotting probability shows a non-monotonic dependence on film thickness, peaking near the bulk radius of gyration, while layer-resolved analysis confirms that wall effects dictate local structural changes.
method: The research employs Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations and Monte Carlo methods, utilizing specialized codes for knot detection (Alexander polynomial) and entanglement analysis (Z1+ code).
model_or_system: The system consists of polymer chains confined between two parallel walls (thin films). The model incorporates harmonic bonds, repulsive WCA interactions, and a Kratky-Porod potential to account for chain stiffness.
key_observables: Knotting probability ($P_{	ext{knot}}$), entanglement length ($	ext{N}_{	ext{entang}}$), and layer-resolved components of the squared radius of gyration ($\langle R_g^2 angle$).
important_parameters: Film thickness ($h$), stiffness parameter ($B$), and the bulk radius of gyration ($R_{g,	ext{bulk}}$) serve as critical length scales.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on developing an integration scheme to extrapolate global film properties from layer-resolved data, though deviations are noted for very thin films.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the non-monotonic dependence of $P_{	ext{knot}}$ on $h$ for flexible chains, and show layer-resolved profiles of knotting and $\langle R_g^2 angle$ components near the confining walls.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the effect of confinement on topology, first establishing the general $h$-dependence, then using layer-resolved analysis to map local wall effects, and finally investigating the role of chain flexibility (stiffness $B$).
why_interesting: While focused on classical polymer physics, the study of topological constraints and confinement effects provides a rich analogy for understanding entanglement in quantum many-body systems or the topological stability of quantum states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 1: Knotting probability Pknot, entanglement length Nentang and normalized trefoil knot lengths Ltref/Lcontour of confined films as a function of the film thickness h. Thicknesses are normalized by the polymers’ bulk radii of gyration Rg,bulk and all points correspond to a bulk monomer density of ρ = 0.68 σ−3. (a) The knotting probability exhibits a maximum at a film thickness of h ≈0.6 · Rg,bulk for all chain lengths. The knotting probabilities of bulk melts for each chain length are indicated by h = ∞; the knotting probabilities of the films consistently approach these bulk values. In contrast, the limit h →0 leads to decreasing knotting probabilities. The inset shows the...

Figure 3: Knotting probability Pknot of confined films as a function of the film thickness h at various chain stiffnesses B. All points correspond to a monomer number of N = 512 and monomer density of ρ = 0.68 σ−3. The knotting probability does not exhibit clear maxima in the implemented thicknesses when stiffness B ̸= 0 is introduced.
